Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580
DOI 10.1007/s11042-016-4181-9




Automatic identification of cattle using muzzle point
pattern: a hybrid feature extraction
and classification paradigm

Santosh Kumar 1 & Sanjay Kumar Singh 1


Received: 8 May 2016 / Revised: 21 September 2016 / Accepted: 18 November 2016 /
Published online: 20 December 2016
# Springer Science+Business Media New York 2016



Abstract Animal biometrics is an emerging research field that develops quantified method-
ologies for representing and detecting the visual appearances of animal based on generic
features and primary biometric characteristics. The identification of individual cattle is an
important issue for classification of different breads, their registration, traceability, health
management, and verification of false insurance claim throughout the world. To solve these
major problems, the muzzle (nose) point image pattern of cattle is a suitable and primary
biometric characteristic for the recognition of cattle. The recognition of muzzle point images is
similar to the recognition of minutiae points in the human fingerprints. In this paper, we
propose a hybrid feature extraction approach for the automatic recognition and classification of
cattle breeds based on captured muzzle point image pattern features using low- cost camera.
The major contributions of this research is based on following aspects: (1) preparation of
muzzle point image dataset, (2) extraction of salient set of features and (3) K-nearest neighbour
(K-NN), Fuzzy-KNN, Decision Tree (DT), Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), Probabilistic
Neural Network (PNN), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) and Naive Bayes classification models
are applied to recognition, and classification of individual cattle using their muzzle point
pattern. This paper, therefore, demonstrates the automatic recognition and classification of
livestock using the set of extracted muzzle point image features. The experimental results show
that proposed hybrid feature extraction and recognition approach outperforms the current state-
of- the art method for the identification of individual cattle using their muzzle point image
pattern.




* Santosh Kumar
  santosh.rs.cse@iitbhu.ac.in
* Sanjay Kumar Singh
  sks.cse@iitbu.ac.in


1
    Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu
    University), Varanasi -221005, India
26552                                                   Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


Keywords Animal biometrics . Muzzle point pattern . Cattle recognition . Feature extraction .
Classification . Texture feature


1 Introduction

The animal biometric system is a pattern recognition based system for recognizing species or
the different animal. It is an emerging research field that design and develops the quantified
methodologies for recognition, representation, and detection of visual appearances of different
species, individual, behaviours, morphological patterns, and biometrics characteristics [28].
For example, SLOOP [16] is an animal biometric-based identification system for recognizing
the different species or individual animal based on their visual features and morphological
image patterns [16].
    For the animal recognition, animal biometric systems extract salient sets of features of
visual appearances, morphological image pattern and biometric characteristics from the species
or individual [26, 34]. The obtained extracted the set of notable features compared against
feature(s) stored in the database and executes an action based on the result of comparisons [28,
34]. Animal biometrics works at the intersection between computer vision, pattern recognition,
ecology, image processing and cognitive sciences [35, 49].
    Animal biometrics-based identification of cattle is an opportunity for the realization of the
variety of useful applications, such as registration of livestock, tracking and health monitoring,
improved security against manipulation and swapping of animal and automatic verification of
false insurance claims of animals.
    Classical animal recognition methodologies have severe problems with recognition, regis-
tration and traceability of animals. It controls and outbreaks the critical animal diseases in the
cattle breed throughout the world because these approaches are manual identification methods.
The classical animal identification approaches are mainly ear-tags, ear-tattoos, freeze-branding
and ear-tips or notches for identification of individual animals. The classical animal ap-
proaches can be easily fraudulent, duplication and forged by modify the unique numbers in
the labelled ear-tags. The amendment of tagged numbers provides a way for manipulation or
swapping of animals [49]. Therefore, classical animal identification methodologies always fail
to cater a required level of protection to livestock animal [34].
    According to the survey of Wagyu Registry Association (WRA), the registration
process of cattle (breeds) is required to be done at the age of 5–6 months of cattle.
After 13–14 months of cattle birth, cattle breeds are applicable for breeding, produc-
tion and marketing purpose [37].
    The major advantages of traceability process of livestock animal are to verify the parentage
or ownership of the livestock animal and to recognition the widespread of critical animal
diseases. It also helps to ensure the food safety and validate sources, productions, processes
and exports throughout the world [1, 6, 17].
    The animal recognition using capture-mark-recapture based approaches have been carried
out by applying the different visual marking schemes for the registration and identification of
animals. It also includes various artificial marking techniques, such as ear-tagging techniques,
freeze-branding, embedding microchips in their body, RFID, and tracking of different animals
[20].
    The animal recognition approaches are intrusive to varying degrees and inefficient to
implement for the enormous population of animal [16]. Thus, there is a need to explore the
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                  26553


alternative approach for cattle recognition that mitigates the major challenging problems of
classical animal identification for monitoring and tracking of animals across the world and
provide the efficient solutions to traditional animal recognition based systems and classical
livestock framework based systems. Therefore, animal biometrics offers a new paradigm to
design and develop a non-invasive, cost-effective, robust and automatic recognition system for
the identification, and verification of individual animal using their unique and immutable
primary biometric characteristics. For example, iconic coat patterns (joint stripes markers on
zebras) [1] Tigers, or spot pattering on cheetahs, the facial image of animals [34] and muzzle
point image pattern [49] of cattle are unique and immutable first animal biometric character-
istics for recognition of individual animal.
    Baranova et al. [5] studied the dermatoglyphics about the granule, ridges and vibrissae from
the various cattle breeds. Based on the report, they concluded that the muzzle point image
pattern of livestock (cattle) has unique and discriminatory biometric characteristics. The
muzzle biometric features consist of two essential attributes: (1) beads pattern and (2) ridges
patterns for the recognition the different cattle. The basic structures of beads patterns in the
muzzle images are not the uniform pattern. These patterns are similar to islands. On the other
hand, the ridges structure is a unique and immutable biometric pattern which is similar to
recognition of minutiae point of the human fingerprint. The shape of ridge pattern is similar to
rivers. It separates the bead patterns. The beads and ridges pattern play a significant role in
recognizing the individual cattle [49, 63]. Based on these biometric characteristics, cattle
recognition system provides efficient, robust, cost-effective, and suitable biometric identifier
based recognition of missed, swapped, false insurance claims, re- allocation of slaughter
houses, control and outbreaks of critical diseases of the large population of cattle throughout
the world [31, 49].
    To the best, our knowledge, muzzle point image pattern of cattle is a suitable and most
discriminatory primary biometric characteristic for the recognition of individual cattle [5, 46].
In this paper, we propose a hybrid texture feature extraction approach for recognition and
classification of individual cattle using their muzzle point image pattern database. The muzzle
point image pattern consist of a rich skin texture information and distinct features, such as
beads and ridge pattern [20]. The cattle recognition is an emerging research field and very few
researches have been done hitherto for the recognition and classification of cattle. The
proposed cattle recognition system also applied for the biometric enrolment, registration and
tracking of individual cattle [49, 54].
    The proposed hybrid texture feature extraction approach extracts the texture features from
the database of muzzle point image pattern. The feature vectors of query muzzle point image
are matched with stored set of features in the database for the recognition and classification of
cattle.
    The motivation of proposed muzzle point recognition approach for cattle is to achieve
higher resilience towards classical animal recognition based approaches and livestock man-
agement systems. For the recognition of cattle, hybrid texture feature extraction based muzzle
point recognition algorithm is proposed for identifying individual cattle, which is suitable,
robust, automatic and non-invasive approach for recognition and verification of individual
cattle.
    Only a few researchers in cattle biometrics have been done and these researches proven that
muzzle point image pattern is first biometric features for recognition of cows. The proposed
system is a non-invasive, robust, fraud-proof, easy to acquire, cost-effective and fundamental
biometric characteristics based recognition system for cattle.
26554                                                   Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


   In the available literature, authors and researchers have done recognition of individual cattle
using muzzle print images. The muzzle print images of cattle were captured on A-5 paper with
blue ink by using manual methods. After the acquisition, muzzle print images are required to
convert into 300 dpi (dot per inch) resolutions of muzzle print images for recognition of cattle.
The muzzle print images based cattle identification is not an efficient approach because these
procedures produce false acceptance rate. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first
research work on automatic recognition and classification of individual cattle using proposed
hybrid texture features of muzzle point image.

&   Major Contributions:

    Followings are the major contributions of our research:

1. In this paper, we propose a hybrid texture feature extraction based approach for the
   recognition and classification of individual cattle using a muzzle point image pattern as
   a primary biometric characteristic.
2. The distinct features such as beads and ridges are extracted from the muzzle point images
   using texture feature extraction approaches such as, Haralick texture features techniques),
   morphology, and shape based features, Histogram of Oriented Gradient (HOG), Wavelet
   feature, colour Feature [56], Tamura feature, Law Texture Energy (LTE), Speeded Up
   Robust Features (SURF), Local Binary Pattern (LBP), and Fuzzy-Local Binary Pattern
   (Fuzzy-LBP) from the images of muzzle point pattern database.
3. A database of muzzle point image pattern of 500 cattle (subjects) is prepared with 20
   megapixel camera from the Department of Diary, and Husbandry, Institute of Agriculture
   Sciences (I. A. S.), Banaras Hindu University (B.H.U.), Varanasi-India-221,005. The size
   of muzzle database is 5000 muzzle point image pattern (i.e., 500 subjects 10 muzzle
   images of each subject).

    The rest part of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 illustrates the related works in
the field of classical animal recognition, and classification approaches. Section 3 deals with the
material and methods for the recognition of cattle breeds using their muzzle point images.
Section 4 illustrates the proposed muzzle point recognition approach for identifying individual
cattle in brief. Section 5 presents the feature extraction and matching of muzzle point images in
detail. The classification of extracted texture features of muzzle point images, tuning strategies
for applied classifiers, and statistical analysis of extracted texture features of muzzle point
images for the recognition and classification is presented in Section 6. Section 7 presents
experimental design, and results along with their detail analysis. Finally, Section 8 is dedicated
to the conclusion and future works.


2 Related work

The classical animal identification based approaches can be divided into several groups- (1)
Permanent Recognition Methodology (PRM), (2) Semi-permanent Recognition Methodology
(SRM) [34] and (3) Temporary Recognition Methodology (TRM) [33]. The ear-tattoos, ear
tagging, microchips and freeze branding are known as PRM recognition based invasive
techniques [42]. In spite of that, as reported in [3], these identification approaches suffer from
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                   26555


the number of limitations - (1) ear-tips or notches are not suitable for large-scale animal
recognition in the traditional identification systems because it includes various metal clips and
plastic tags for the recognition of individual cattle in a herd. However, ear-tips and ear-tags
based systems provide multiple infections to animals [27, 67].
   According to authors, Edwards and Johnston [27], about more than 60% of the animals
(e.g. cattle, sheep or goat) suffered from the ear damage due to the embedded plastic ear-tags in
their ear because, these ear-tags keep the local inflammation, pronounced thickening, mild
sepsis, and traces of haemorrhaging. Moreover, there are major drawbacks of ear-tagging
based identification of cattle such as (1) ear-tags can be lost easily because tag tearing through
the ear, (2) ear-tags disintegrates the ear of cattle for long term usages and (3) during the
embedding of ear-tags into ear of cattle provides more inflammatory response, pain and
discomfort, mostly if the ear is handled when reading the tags with RFID reader or scanner
[33]. While SRM based animal identification method includes several techniques, such as
sketching of the pattern of cattle or fur and id-collar to identify animals. Moreover, Radio
Frequency based Identification (RFID) and paints or dying are known as TRM identification
methodologies for animal recognition [34, 64].
   Few artificial marking based approaches such as, freeze-branding, ear-tips or notches and
embedded microchips have been applied for making their hides for the identification of
animals for thousands of years [18]. Originally the identification process was useful to
particularly important for animals, e.g. horses, cattle, zebra, penguin and Bewick’s swans.
The simplest one to conceive is manual identification based approaches; however, its high
recall comes at exceptional cost.
   The sketching pattern can also be applied to recognition cattle of the broken colour of
breeds (e.g., Guernsey, Holsteins, and Ayrshires) [49]. However, it needs a high drawing
ability of individual for sketching; therefore, it lacks in standard image quality and affects the
identification process of cattle. It is not applicable to solid collared breeds (e.g., Red Poll,
Milking Shorthorn, and Brown Swiss breed) as they need some discriminating based artificial
marking methods known as tattoos, ear-tagging on the cattle ear. However, these techniques
damage the ear of cattle for the long term usages.
   According to Wardrope [67], Johnston and Edwards [27], ear tagging based method is not
suitable for identification of cattle due to the label of ear-tags can also be eventually damaged.
The ear of cattle corrupted due to long-term usage [5]. The classical cattle identification
methodologies are illustrated in Table 1.
   In the available literature, recognition approaches for cattle using muzzle print
images (nose print) are reported using blue ink and lifted on A-5 paper [45]. Before,
acquisition of the print image of muzzle pattern, cattle was tightly bounded with
ropes. Recently, the research focuses on more advancement in cattle recognition have
led to a new paradigm for identification of cattle using muzzle print image [45, 49]. The
impacted (print) image of muzzle pattern consists of two important attributes known as bridge
pattern and ridge pattern, respectively [62].
   According to Baranov et al. [5], muzzle dermatoglyphics (i.e., ridges, granola, and vibris-
sae) from various races are different. In a similar direction, Mishra et al. [46] proposed a
method for the recognition of cattle breeds using their beads and ridge features of muzzle print
images. In the similar direction, Minagawa et al. [45] proposed a method for cattle identifi-
cation using muzzle print and evaluated performances using filtering techniques for analysis of
muzzle image, binary transformation processes and morphological approaches (i.e., thinning
operation). They reported the Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.419, respectively.
26556                                                          Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580

Table 1 Classical methodology for cattle identification

Identification Approaches/Attributes   PRM                                      SRM          TRM

                                       ET    MC     Ear-Tipping/notching   FB   IDC   ETT    SK    RFID

Reliability                            Me    VH     H                      VH   L     L      L     VL
Cost                                   Me    VH     L                      Me   L     L      VL    VL
Visibility                             VL    NA     Me                     H    VH    VH     VH    NA
Longevity                              H     VH     VH                     H    L     L      VL    L
Risk of harm                           L     VL     Me                     L    L     VH     VL    H
Accuracy                               H     VH     NA                     L    H     L      VL    H
Uniqueness                             H     VH     NA                     L    H     Me     VL    VH
Database Required                      H     VH     NA                     L    L     Me     NA    VH

Where PRM Permanent Recognition Methodology, SRM Semi-permanent Recognition Methodology, TRM
Temporary Recognition Methodology, ET Ear-Tattoo, MC Microchip, FB Freeze-Branding, IDC ID-Collar, ETT
Ear-Tagging, SK Sketching pattern, C Cost, Me = Medium, VH Very High, H High, L Low, VL Very Low, NA
Not Available notations are given in Table 1.


   Barry et al. [6] also proposed a framework for the recognition of cattle using images of
muzzle print. It is unlike Minagawa et al. [45]. The authors reported the 241 False Non-Match
Rates (FNMR) over 560 Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR) and 5197 false matches over
12,160 impostor matching closely with the same value of EER of 0.429, respectively.
   Kim et al. [29] proposed a method to recognize the Japanese black cattle using their pixel
intensity of cattle faces [29]. The author of [9] proposed a facial representation model for the
recognition of cattle based on local binary pattern (LBP) texture features of facial images of
cattle. In [48], authors proposed cattle recognition approach based on Speeded Up Robust
feature (SURF) descriptor features. This approach has reported the experimental results on
minimum images of data-set which captured on A-5 paper with blue inked only from 4 cattle
breeds for the recognition purpose.
   In the similar direction, author of [49] proposed a matching refinement technique in Scale
Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) descriptor approach for the recognition of cattle on the
database of 160 muzzle print images. They have computed the matching scores of the key
points of muzzle impact images by applying matching refinement technique in SIFT approach.
However, the matching refinement approach has had the performance compared to the original
SIFT approach with the value of EER being equal to 0.0167.
   Awad et al. [4] proposed a framework for the cattle recognition using SIFT descriptor
approach to localize and detect the interesting keypoints of beads and ridges in the muzzle print
images for the identification of cattle. In this experiment, size of muzzle print image database is
only 90 muzzle images (e.g. 6 (cattle) × 15 (each cattle has 15 muzzle images) = 90 images).
   In the same direction, Tharwat et al. [62] proposed a muzzle image based cattle recognition
approach using local texture descriptor technique, such as local binary pattern based texture
extraction algorithm for the extraction of local texture features from the muzzle point images.
The main advantages of local binary pattern algorithm are suitable invariant to rotation of the
muzzle point images. The local discriminant analysis technique is applied for the reduction of
dimension of extracted texture features and classifies the discriminate features between
different classes; therefore the proposed approach is capable for improving the accuracy of
cattle recognition in the proposed framework for cattle recognition.
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                 26557


    Recently, author Tharwat et al. [62] proposed a muzzle print image based recognition
approach of cattle using local texture descriptor based technique, such as LBP texture
algorithm for the extraction of local texture features from the muzzle print images. The
RANdom SAmple Consensus (RANSAC) technique is used to mitigating the outliers from
the muzzle image which is incorporated with the SIFT algorithm for the improvement of
reliability and robustness of their proposed identification approach for cattle. The six images
for each subject (cattle) are used as training muzzle images in the experiment and total size of
database is 90 muzzle images (6 (cattle) × 15 (each subject has 15 images)) = 90). They
achieved more than 90% accuracy of cattle recognition. The major shortcoming of this
proposed approach is to take more processing time during recognition of cattle.


3 Material and methods

This section deals with the details of database of muzzle point image pattern of cattle used as
input image in proposed approach illustrated in the following sub sections:

3.1 Database preparation and description

The database of muzzle point image was prepared using, a 20-megapixel camera to capture the
images from the Department of Animal Husbandry and Dairy, Institute of Agriculture Sciences
(I. A. S.), Bananas Hindu University (B. H. U), Varanasi, India-221,005.
    The preparation of database has been done in two different sessions under different
unconstrained environments. The size of muzzle point image database is 5000 (e. g., 500
subjects ×10 images per subjects) and size of each muzzle point image is 200 × 200 pixel.
Some samples of muzzle point image from the cattle database are shown in Fig. 1. Based on
the observation of muzzle point image pattern from the 500 cattle breeds, no one of pair of
image pattern has the similar pattern as distinguished features [5]. The muzzle point image
contains two important patterns in the muzzle images namely, (1) breads pattern, and (2) ridges
pattern which is shown in Figs. 2 and 3, respectively.




Fig. 1 Some muzzle point image pattern of cattle from database
26558                                                             Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580




Fig. 2 Beads and ridges of the muzzle point pattern of cattle from the database


   Table 2 illustrates the composition of the muzzle point images of cattle breeds for the
experiments. The database preparation of muzzle point images is taken from the four major
races (breeds) of cattle. The database of muzzle point images is prepared from 500 individual
cattle (subjects). Each subject has 10 muzzle images and size of images is 200 × 200 pixels.


4 Proposed muzzle point pattern recognition approach of cattle

In this section, we illustrate the proposed approach for recognition and classification of
cattle in detail. The various steps in the proposed hybrid feature extraction approach
are described. The schematic description of proposed hybrid feature approach for
cattle identification and classification is shown in Fig. 4. For the recognition of cattle
using proposed approach, various stages have involved namely- pre-processing, en-
hancement of images of cattle to mitigate the specific kind of noises of images and
improve the image quality.
    In the proposed approach, muzzle point images are used by performing pre-processing,
enhancements, segmentation of muzzle images for the extraction of features. The primary
objective of pre-processing and enhancement algorithms has applied the enhancement of
muzzle images before analysis of extracted texture features and better representation in the




Fig. 3 Some sample images of muzzle point image patterns of cattle which are used in the calf registration a at
the age of 2 months for advanced calf registration, b illustrates registration of cattle using muzzle images at the
age of 14 months
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                         26559

Table 2 Description of muzzle point images pattern database

Breeds (Races)                   No. of subjects (cattle)        No. of images (10 images of each cattle)

Balinese cow                     150                             1500
Hybrid Ongole cow                150                             1500
Holestein Friesian cow           100                             1000
Cross Breed Cow                  100                             1000



feature space. The muzzle point images are pre-processed before the extraction of feature, and
matching process in the proposed approach.
   In the proposed approach, the motivation behind to apply the pre-processing and enhance-
ment techniques is to enhance an of low-quality muzzle point images into standard images by
reducing the noises as artefacts from the captured muzzle point images. Therefore, Contrast
Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) [73, 76] technique is applied for the
enhancement process of muzzle point images. After enhancement process, colour K-means
segmentation algorithm is applied to find the Region of Interest (ROI) from the input image of
muzzle point pattern [30, 52, 70, 75].
   After pre-processing steps, segmentation algorithms are applied to segment the muzzle
point images into different regions or segments. Each muzzle point segment is employed to
find the Region of Interest (ROI) and choose the more discriminative information area of
muzzle point features. After that, texture feature extraction algorithms are applied to extract the
texture features from each segmented muzzle point images. Finally, extracted features of
muzzle images are classified using different classification models to recognition the muzzle
point image pattern for identifying cattle.
   After segmentation of muzzle point image pattern, texture features of muzzle image (e.g.,
rich texture information, and distinct features, such as beads, and ridge pattern) (as shown in
Fig. 2) are extracted using texture feature approaches. The texture feature extraction techniques
are Haralick texture features techniques [13, 23, 24, 39], morphology based features, shape
based features [12], Histogram of Oriented Gradient (HOG) features [14], Wavelet feature
[10], Colour features [60, 65], Tamuras feature [55], Laws Texture Energy (LTE) [2], Local
Binary pattern texture features [50, 68], and Fuzzy-Local Binary Pattern (Fuzzy-LBP) [21].




Fig. 4 Block diagram of the proposed automatic recognition system of cattle based on muzzle point image
pattern
26560                                                          Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


These features are applied to extract the texture feature from the database of muzzle point
image pattern. Finally, the K-nearest neighbourhood (K-NN) [66, 72], Fuzzy-K-NN [7], Radial
Basis Probability Network (RBPN) [59], Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN) [38], Decision
Tree (DT) [59], Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) [51], Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) [8, 61]
and Naive Bayes based [44] classification models are applied to classify the extracted muzzle
texture feature for classification, and recognition of cattle. The various stages involved in the
proposed approach are discussed below:

4.1 Pre-processing and enhancement of muzzle point image

The pre-processing is a mandatory step for feature extraction, and matching process for
recognition of objects. The objectives of the pre-processing step are to mitigate the noises,
and other artefacts, such as gray scaling, and cropping of muzzle point images. The existence
of noises that occurred due to the presence of low illumination, poor image quality, blurriness
and pose variation due to head movement of cattle during database acquisition. These artefacts
are manifested during acquisition the phase of muzzle image database. Therefore, we have
applied the Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) image processing
technique to mitigate the noises, and enhancement of muzzle point images [58, 73, 76]. The
CLAHE technique is applied for the improvement of image quality and enhancement of beads
and ridges pattern in muzzle point images. After the enhancement, it provides a better contrast
to muzzle image to highlight the segmented Region of Interest (ROI) in the muzzle point
images as tested through experimentation. Figure 5 illustrates the pre-processing of muzzle
point image pattern of cattle. The pre-processing of muzzle point image pattern and extraction
of beads and ridges as prominent texture features shown in the Figs. 5 and 6, respectively. The
segmentation and feature extraction are discussed in brief in the next subsection.

4.2 Segmentation of muzzle point image

The image segmentation algorithms are essential to segment the muzzle point image into
required distinct regions of beads and ridges pattern. The muzzle point images consist of rich
texture information (feature) and different colours based discriminating texture features. After
the pre-processing of muzzle images, colour clustering and texture segmentation based
algorithms were examined and applied for the segmentation of muzzle point images.
   The primary motives to use colour clustering and texture segmentation based algorithms are
to preserve the relevant and discriminatory muzzle feature sets (information) for the recogni-
tion and classification process. Therefore, colors K-means and texture segmentation algorithms
are applied to segment the muzzle point images into distinct region of interest [19]. The




Fig. 5 Illustrates pre-processing: a original image, b blurred image, and c enhanced muzzle image
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                            26561




Fig. 6 Pre-processing of beads and ridges from the original images of muzzle point pattern: a input muzzle
image, b blurred muzzle point image pattern, c perform the pre-processing steps to find the beads and ridge
pattern from the muzzle point images and d shows the filtration process of overlapping region between beads,
and ridges pattern


motivation to apply the color K-means based clustering [40] algorithm is that it preserves the
discriminatory colour features from the muzzle point images.
    The segmented muzzle point image pattern is shown in Fig. 7. The Figs. 8, 9 and 10
illustrate the obtained results from the original, ground truth and region of interest (ROI) based
segmentation of muzzle point image pattern using colour- based K-means clustering and
texture segmentation algorithms.
    The segmentation algorithms partition the input images into multiple segments and find out
ROI from each segmented images. However, segmentation algorithms depend on the type of
required discriminating features to be preserved, and extraction of discriminatory information
from particular ROI of segmented muzzle point image pattern. Figure 10 illustrates that segment-
ed of muzzle point image pattern using K-means colour cluster algorithm shown in ((a)-(e)) for
clustersn = 3(i.e., cluster-1, cluster-2, and cluster-3, respectively.) where, (a) original muzzle




Fig. 7 a original muzzle point image b segmentation of muzzle point image pattern
26562                                                           Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580




Fig. 8 a original muzzle point image pattern, b segmented muzzle image with K-means colour based segmen-
tation approach


image, (b) image labelled by cluster index, ((c)-(e)) present beads and ridges of muzzle point
image as required object attributes in the cluster-1, cluster-2, and cluster-3, respectively.

   &    Minima Selection and Region Merging

    The watershed segmentation algorithms are applied to partition into regions of the object in
given image. The major disadvantage of watershed algorithm is that, it begins from the
initialization of every minimum of the gradient of the image; it always generates an excessive
over-segmentation. Hence, in this paper, a dynamics algorithm is apply with segmentation
technique for the selection of minima value using suppression of irrelevant minima image
gradient, and keep rest is used to initialize the markers selection of watershed [41].
    The dynamics algorithm mitigates the over segmentation problem by reducing the insuffi-
cient minima of partitioned ROIs of muzzle images into different classes. The unsupervised
watershed segmentation algorithm provides an efficient way for merging process of local
minima values for better segmentation of muzzle point images. Finally, merging the neigh-
boring regions of different classes of muzzle image is processed as follows:

1. In the merging procedure, it applies a strategy to combine the smaller regions of the
   segmented image (applicable on RGB values). The values of RGB produces the regions




Fig. 9 Illustrates segmentation of muzzle point image pattern: a original muzzle images, ground truth, and b - e
illustrates the finding out a ROI using texture segmentation algorithm
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                              26563




Fig. 10 Illustrates the segmented muzzle point image pattern using K-means colour cluster based segmentation
algorithms


   keeping areas of regions which is significant to evaluation statistically significant as well
   as textural parameters.
2. In the second case, it merges into bigger regions of segmented images using defined stopping
   criteria (e.g., difference between of pixel intensities, textural and histogram differences). The
   segmented muzzle point images using watershed algorithm is shown in Fig. 11.


5 Feature extraction and matching

The feature is an important characteristic to encode the given image pattern into the set of
discriminatory measurable information or values. The feature extraction is a pre-processing process
in the proposed cattle recognition approach, which is responsible for texture analysis, and classifica-
tion of image pattern based on discriminating sets features. The eventual objective of this section is to
articulate extraction of the feature vector from each image of muzzle point pattern database of cattle.
    After, pre-processing, and segmentation of muzzle point images, texture features are extracted
from the muzzle point image [52, 74]. The muzzle point image has rich texture features in the
form of beads and ridges pattern. Therefore, we have applied eight texture features extraction
techniques to extract the texture features from the muzzle images. The eight texture feature




Fig. 11 Illustrates of the watershed based segmentation: selection of region of interest (ROI) from the muzzle
point image pattern database
26564                                                           Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


extraction approaches are illustrates namely, Haralick texture features algorithms [13, 23, 24, 39],
morphology based features [12, 25], shape based features [12], Histogram of Oriented Gradient
(HOG) features [14, 57],Wavelet feature [10], Colour based features [60, 65], Tamuras feature
[55], Laws Texture Energy (LTE) [2], Local Binary Pattern texture features [50, 68] and Fuzzy-
Local Binary Pattern (Fuzzy-LBP) [21, 53, 68]. The ranges of extracted sets of texture feature are
shown in Table 3. Table 3 illustrates the distribution of name of feature type, and number of
features is selected for the identification and classification of muzzle point image.
   Finally, classification is performed on the extracted texture features. In this paper, K-nearest
neighbourhood (K-NN) [66], Fuzzy-K-NN [7], Radial Basis Probability Network (RBPN)
[59], Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN) [38], Decision Tree (DT) [59], Gaussian Mixture
Model (GMM) [51], Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) [8, 61] and Naive Bayes based [44]
classifier models are applied for classification, and recognition of cattle.

5.1 Encoding of texture features of muzzle point images

A feature extraction and representation descriptor are essentially well-defined functions that
convert an input image or image regions into a corresponding feature vector through encoding
process. The LBP is a feature descriptor based feature extraction and encoding algorithm. The
extracted muzzle features are invariant to monotonic changes into the intensity in the gray level
domain. However, appearance based recognition algorithm solves variations in the pixel intensity
in a given image are solved by appearance based recognition algorithm. These variations occur
due to low illumination conditions and other outdoor environments. Therefore, extracted local
features are extracted and encoded by its descriptors with size(m × n)from the neighbor pixel of
given images. The length of extracted feature vector is known as the size of a feature descriptor.
The LBP and fuzzy-LBP [53] texture algorithms are applied for the local texture features from the
muzzle images, extracted features are encoded to obtain a descriptor [32, 36, 47].
   In the available literature, the most of the early research works on texture classification and
recognition apply the hard assignment. The quantization for this purpose, however, defined
assignments can lead to cater a quantization and reconstruction error space during classifica-
tion, and recognition in the feature space. Instead, we apply the soft quantization based
assignment technique by representing the distribution of texture feature using a discriminant
Fisher vector for the classification of extracted texture features from the muzzle point image
database [19]. The fisher vectors apply the Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) for the deriva-
tion of a probabilistic representation of these texture features of muzzle point images. The main

Table 3 Illustration of various texture features extracted from the muzzle point image pattern and their ranges

Name of features                                                        Number of features     Range F1–F186

[28] Haralick texture features [4, 73, 76]                              22                     F1–F22
[16] Morphology and shape feature [52, 75]                              10                     F23-F32
[26] Histogram of Oriented Gradient (HOG) [30]                          36                     F33-F69
[34] Wavelet Feature [70]                                               32                     F70-F102
[49] Colour features [13, 24]                                            6                     F103-F109
[35] Tamuras’s feature [39]                                              3                     F110-F112
[37] Law’s Texture Features [23]                                        16                     F113-F129
[17] Fuzzy-Local Binary Pattern (FLBP) [10, 12, 14, 60] [8, 44, 61]     56                     F-130-F186
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                      26565


motivation behind to apply the Fisher vector is that it cater to get a fixed length and compact
image representation for the recognition for a set of arbitrary number of local texture features
which is captured from the muzzle images.
    In this paper, the main objective of encoding technique is to capture the most significant
differences between the first and second order statistical features, image descriptors and the
GMM centres. The calculated differences of the higher order statistics are learned for the
robust classification, and recognition as compared to other available encoding algorithms, such
as histograms, kernel code book. The Fisher vector formulations are described in brief as
follows. Moreover, detailed discussion about fisher vector formulation and detailed analysis
can be found in the references [43] and [15]. The local features are modelled using GMM
model which is defined as:
                                 .  X      K   . X
                               p d θ ¼         p d μk ;         πk                           ð1Þ
                                             k¼1              k

                        K
   Where, pðd=θÞ ¼ ∑ pðd=μk ; ∑Þ is a multivariate Gaussian distribution with mean(μk) co-
                       k¼1          k
variance matrix ð∑Þ (assumed to be diagonal co-variance matrix), πkis the mixing coefficient
                   k
of the Gaussian component, and θ ¼ ðπ1 ; μ1 ; ∑1 ;π2 ; μ2 ; ∑2 ;::::::πK ; μK ; ∑K Þ is the vector of
the parameters for model.
   Here Kis the total number of Gaussian components assumed to be present while modelling
the feature distribution. The parameters of GMM are learned using Expectation Maximization
(EM) using the features from the training samples. We have applied LBP, a texture feature
descriptor based algorithm for classification and recognition of muzzle point image pattern of
cattle because it is faster to compute, and robust to blurriness, pose and illumination changes.
   The corresponds to gray level of the centre pixel and present to gray level of the p equally
spaced pixels on the circle of radius R (R ≥ 0), wherec = (1, 2, 3, ..N). The binary code
generation of LBP for muzzle point image pattern is shown in Fig. 12, respectively.


6 Classifications

To establish the classification of a given probe (test image), a multi-class classifiers are used to
classify the feature vectors, we have used finally, the K-nearest neighbourhood (KNN) [66],
Fuzzy-K-NN [7], Radial Basis Probability Network (RBPN) [59], Decision Tree (DT) [59],
Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) [51], Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN) [38], Multilayer




Fig. 12 Illustration of LBP binary codes obtained by comparing centre pixel (thresholding) with its
neighbouring pixels and transform into decimal code
26566                                                   Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


Perceptron (MLP) [8, 61] and Naive Bayes based [44] classifier models are used for classi-
fication and recognition of cattle from the images database of muzzle point pattern. A brief
description about used classifiers are below:

1. K-nearest neighbour (K-NN)

    The K-nearest neighbour (K-NN) [65] is a classification technique in which a feature vector
is assigned. For K-NN classification, the numbers of nearest neighbour (K) were set to 5 and
Euclidean distance matrix and the Bnearest^ rule to decide how to classify the probe images
(testing) were used.

2. Fuzzy K-nearest neighbour (K-NN)

  The fuzzy K-nearest neighbour (K-NN) is a classification algorithm which allocates class
membership to a sample vector rather than assignment of the vector to a general class [55].

3. Radial Basis Function (RBF)

    In radial basis probabilistic based neural network based classification techniques, distances
from the test feature vector to the training feature vectors are determined by the first neural
network layer known as radial basis layer. The next layer is a competitive layer. It summed up
the distance vectors for each input classes and generate a vector of probabilities considered as
its output. Further, the Bcomplete^ transfer functions are exploited in this layer then choose the
maximum of these probabilities to evaluate the class of the test data [2, 50].

4. Decision Tree (DT)

   Decision Tree is ensemble based classification technique for classifying data. It consists of a
set nodes and edges which organized in a hierarchical fashion. A set of rules of the different
classes are derived from the tree that is constructed using the input features from the training
data [50]. These rules are used to classify and predict the class of the test (query data).

5. Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)

    A Gaussian mixture model is a parametric based probabilistic model that assumes all the
data points are generated from a mixture of a finite number of Gaussian distributions with
unknown parameters. The GMM classifier model uses the maximum likelihood algorithm for
fitting GMM to the training datasets. The trained GMM classifier has been evaluated for test
data [21, 66, 72].

6. Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN) Classifier

   Probabilistic neural network (PNN) [68] is a classification technique based on neural
network. It works feed forward neural network and derived from Bayesian network and statistical
algorithms known as Kernel Fisher Discriminant analysis (KFDA). The motivation behind to
apply the PNN based classifier in this research is that it is very much faster and yields accurate
classification rate as compared to well-known back-propagation paradigm [21, 66].
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                          26567


7. Multi Layer Perceptron Classifier

   The Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) is a feed-forward neural network based classification
technique for classifying and analysing images patterns. It has been used an extensively in
classification and regression analysis of data. It consists of multiple layers of simple, two states
sigmoid processing elements or neurons known as node that interacted using weighted con-
nections. A comprehensive working principle to the Multilayer perceptron can be found in [7].

8. Naïve Bayes Classifier

   The Naive Bayes is a simple probabilistic technique for constructing classifiers based on
Bayes’ theorem with strong (naive) independence hypothesis between the extracted sets of
input features ) of given datasets (D), where X is the feature space. The classifier models that
assign class labels, to problem instances, represented as vectors of feature values (D) [38, 59].


6.1 Classifier tuning strategy

We have performed the classification on extracted texture features using K-NN, Fuzzy KNN,
Radial Basis Probability Network (RBPN), Decision Tree (DT), Gaussian Mixture Model
(GMM), Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) classification
techniques. For the performance measure, the best classifiers among the above classification
models are chosen on the basis of same training and testing of sets of muzzle point feature (data).
To obtain the higher accuracy, the parameters of the different classifiers are tuned. In this research
work, K-NN and Fuzzy K-NN classifiers cater the better performance as compared to other
classification techniques [72, 76–78]. The classifier techniques have performed better recognition
of cattle by classifying and recognizing the features of extracted muzzle images pattern.
    In the fuzzy classifier strategy, Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) consists of conditional if-then
rules that show a relationship between the input and output fuzzy sets. The different clusters
are set fixed using defined radii in the FIS. For the clusters, the radii are defined as a vector that
provides centres range of inferences in the each of the feature space and to be supposed as
feature sets which is assigned within a defined unit feature space area (hyper box).
    The ranges of suitable radii values of different clusters always have a range of0.2 − 0.5,
where, radii are0.2 ≤ radii ≤ 0.5. The range values play a significant role to find a few large
clusters of extracted texture features of the muzzle point images. In this research work, a radii
value is chosen to0.50for the classification of texture features of muzzle point and their
analysis. The input membership function is chosen as Gaussian distribution function and a
linear function is chosen as output membership function for the fuzzy classifier.
    In the case of PNN classifier, all biases layers in radial basis neural network are first initialized
   pﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ
to lgð0:05Þ=S where Sis a spread constant radial basis function of PNN. In this experiment, the
maximum classification accuracy is obtained, when value of spread constant isS = 0.50.
    Furthermore, for K-NN classifier strategy, the value of K-NN varied from 2 to 6 in this
experiment. However, at nearest neighbourK = 2, K-NN classifier yields the maximum clas-
sification accuracy for identifying individual cattle using of muzzle point image, the distance
between different classes are evaluated using classical Euclidean distance technique.
    In this experiment, we have chosen majority rule technique [22] and applied for classifying
the extracted texture features of muzzle images. This is simple procedure, where, a sample
26568                                                       Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


point is assigned to the different sets (class) of the majority of K-NN are from [11]. In the case
of GMM classification technique, the centres of clusters are initialized to5for all the respective
groups of muzzle point image database. While, in the Decision Tree (DT) classifier, the parent
and leaf node values of the tree must be tuned for the classification of muzzle point images.
   For the Decision Tree (DT) classification, the parent and leaf node values are initialized to
10 and 1, respectively. The Naive Bayes classifier is a probabilistic based classification
technique that is applied for analysis of extracted texture features of muzzle point images
which are supposed to be independent to give the classes. Therefore, Naive Bayes classifier
selects a small number of parameters that are required to be estimated for the classifying the
texture feature of muzzle point image pattern with small estimator variance (e.g., one-
dimensional kernel density estimation with Gaussian distribution) (shown in Eq. (1)).

6.2 Statistical analysis

In this section, statistical measures of extracting texture features of muzzle point images are
performed in derail for the significant statistically analysis of extracted texture features. It provides
a way to check and validate the significant of extracting texture features whether a given set of
texture features has a discriminating capability among labelled classes (e.g., feature sets) or not.
    For validation of texture features, classical statistical inference based technique, such as One Way
Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) test is performed to test the statistical significance of extracted
texture features (data). It is one of the well-established statistical tests for comparison of more than
population means of feature vectors [69]. The ANOVA test considers the variable (e.g., variance)
within the set of feature groups and translated into variation (i.e., differences) between the groups,
doing same way, taking this into consideration, this procedure caters the more meaningful infor-
mation about how many subjects (groups) belongs to the same groups or not. Finally, action has
taken into consideration for final decision which is based on observed differences. The observed
differences are high that indicates that the extracted sets of features of muzzle point image pattern are
more statistically significant for the classification, and recognition of cattle breeds.

6.3 Performance evaluation

After the statistical measurement of extracted texture of muzzle image using Analysis of
Variance (ANOVA) test [69]. The performance evaluation has been performed after compu-
tations of variations between the set of features of inter-class (within class) and intra-classes
(between classes) of muzzle point image database.
    When variations between the classes of muzzle point pattern features were produced to be
relatively high as compared to variation of within-class of feature of muzzle point images, then
test was taken under consideration of statistically significant of data. It indicates that higher value
of (F) or low-value of (p) shows feature is statistically significant. Therefore, values of(F) and (p)
are calculated from the extracted texture feature of muzzle point images, shown in Table 4.
    In Table 4, some short notations are used as, LTE = Laws Texture Energy, LBP = Local Binary
Pattern, HOG = Histogram of Oriented Gradient and FLBP = Fuzzy Local Binary Pattern.
    In this paper, 186texture features are extracted from the database of muzzle image pattern
for the recognition, and classification of cattle. Some samples of extracted texture features
from the muzzle point images have resulted in a (p)-value which is less than 0.0001 (as shown
in Table 4). It indicates the extracted features are statistically more significance for the
classification, and recognition of extracted texture muzzle point image features.

                                                                                                                                                                 26569
26570                                                   Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


   The four cattle breeds (races) are illustrated in Table 2. The classes of muzzle point image
database are equivalent to cattle breed or race. The number of the group for muzzle point
images is defined as a subset from the whole muzzle point image database.
   Before, the extraction of texture features of muzzle point images, initially muzzle point
image database is divided into three labelled classes known asA,B and C classes. The dataset
size of setA, and B classes is 2000 muzzle point images each 200 subjects×10 images of each
subject and class C consists of 1000 images of muzzle point pattern (e.g., 100
subject×10images of each subject) with size 200 × 200pixels. The characteristic of class A
muzzle images is manifested with poor illumination of 2000 muzzle pattern images pattern, set
B holds blurriness and pose variation based covariates muzzle images pattern of 2000 images
(e.g., 200 subjects ×10 images) and finally, images of set C corresponding to highly diversified
muzzle images with various illumination, blurred and occlusions.
   The main motivation to divided database of muzzle point images into three classes (A, Band
C classes) is to perform the depth level analysis, and computation of more discriminatory sets
of features for the improvement of classification and recognition of muzzle pattern based on
the variations of significant features shown in Table 4. The division of database of muzzle
point image pattern into three sets (classes) such asA,B and C classes, respectively. The
representation of different classes of muzzle point images is shown in Fig. 13.


7 Experimental results and discussion

The experimental results were performed using computer with Intel Core i5-4210 U CPU
running at 2.4GHz, and 200GB of RAM. The size of muzzle point image database is 5000
(e.g., 500 subjects×10 images per subjects). The size of each muzzle point image is 200 ×
200pixel. Some samples of muzzle point images is shown in Fig. 1.
   For the testing and experimentation purposes, a total of 5000 muzzle images from the cattle
database is taken. For the experimental evaluation 5000 muzzle images were segmented into
three classes, such as A, B and C classes of cattle database. The size of each class is2000,
2000(2000 = 200 × 10) images of each subject) and 1000 (1000 = 100 × 10)images of each
subjects) muzzle point images of cattle. In feature extraction phase, eight texture feature
extraction techniques are applied for the extraction of texture features from these classes after
pre-processing and segmented muzzle point images.
   The texture feature extract techniques include Haralick texture features (gray level texture
features) (F1 − F22)morphology and shape features(F23 − F32), Histogram of Oriented Gra-
dient (HOG) (F33 − F69), wavelet features(F70 − F102), colour based features (F103 − F109),


Fig. 13 Distribution of muzzle
point image pattern database under
different conditions
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                                 26571


Tamuras features (F110 − F112), and Laws Texture Energy (F113 − F129) based features,
Fuzzy-Local Binary Pattern (Fuzzy-LBP) and LBP(F130 − F186) based features. Finally a 2-
D matrix of 5000 × 186texture feature matrix was constructed using all the extracted set of
texture features, where 5000 is the muzzle point images pattern in the cattle database and 186
the total number of extracted texture features from the muzzle point images.
    The muzzle point image database was captured from the unconstrained environment (e.g.,
low illumination, poor image quality, pose variation during head movement of cattle), there-
fore, we have texture features extraction techniques to captures the variation of pixel intensity
in the muzzle point images for better recognition, and classification of extracted muzzle
features of cattle [63, 77].
    For the training, six muzzle images of each subject were randomly selected for
training purpose of used classifiers and remaining muzzle point images were applied
for the testing process. The training and testing partitioned database of muzzle point
images are performed five-fold cross validation to test the classifiers, such as K-
nearest neighbour (K-NN) [66], Fuzzy K-NN [7], RBPNN [59], DT [59], PNN [38], GMM [8,
61] and Naive Bayes classifiers [44] to perform the classification, and recognition of muzzle
point images of cattle.

7.1 Experimental analysis

The experimental results of cattle recognition using muzzle point images are summarized in
Tables 5 and 6, respectively. Table 5 illustrates the performances of different classification
algorithms along with their comparative recognition accuracy. In Table 5, first three
columns present the recognition accuracies from the class-A of muzzle point image
pattern. The second columns and third columns present the recognition accuracy by
classifying the extracted texture features of muzzle point images of classes B and C,
respectively. It is also shown the evident from the experimental results, K-NN
classifier yields the better recognition accuracy by classifying individual cattle using
their muzzle point image based features as compared to other classification algorithms.
   Based on the observations of experimental results, K-NN classification technique yields the
accuracy of 95.82% to classify, and recognize individual cattle. The fuzzy-KNN, DT, GMM,

muzzle images

PNN, MLP [8, 61] and Naive Bayes classifiers yield the recognition accuracy of 94.85%,
75.54% (class A), 79.45% accuracy from the class B and 85.76% recognition accuracy from
the class C.
    While GMM based classifier yields the accuracy of 77.63%, 85.67% and 88.47% to
classify the individual cattle from the group A, B and C, respectively. Moreover, PNN based
classifier provides the accuracy of 94.56%, 94.88% and 79.68%from the classes A, B and C to
recognize the cattle, while MLP classifier achieved the classification rates of 67.84%, 77.93%
and 74.43% from the classes A, B and C, respectively. The Naive Bayes classifier yields
77.32%, 73.84% and 71.84% recognition rates of muzzle images pattern for recognizing the
individual cattle.
    The classification accuracy of the K-NN classifier among all classifiers, such as Fuzzy K-
NN, RBF, DT, GMM and PNN yield the maximum classification accuracy of 96.74%.
Therefore, K-NN classifier also has been chosen for classifying the extracted features to
recognition individual cattle in this research work.
    The recognition rate of the muzzle point pattern of cattle from different classes is shown in
Table 6. In this table, the recognition rate of groups (A, B, and C) has been considered in
different cases of muzzle point images pattern database and their recognition accuracies are
illustrated in Table 6.
    We have performed the experimental results under different scenarios such as, rotation and
occluded (e.g., horizontally and vertically occluded part) of muzzle point images for the
recognition and classification of cattle, which is illustrated in subsections 7.2 and 7.3,
respectively.

7.2 Recognition of muzzle point images under different rotation

In this subsection, we have performed the experimental results of muzzle pattern recognition of
cattle based different orientations of muzzle images, and tested against different orientation
during the testing phases. The muzzle images patterns are rotated in the following angles:
(0∘,45∘,90∘,135∘,180∘,225∘,270∘, and315∘). The different orientations of images of muzzle
point pattern are shown in Fig. 14 and recognition accuracy is illustrated in Table 7, respec-
tively. The recognition of cattle based on muzzle point images under different rotation classes
are divided according to rotation angles (in degree) shown in Table 7.
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                              26573




Fig. 14 Some sample images of muzzle point pattern are rotated with different angles
(0∘,45∘,90∘,135∘,180∘,225∘,270∘and315∘)



7.3 Recognition of muzzle image under occlusion

In this section, experimental results and analysis are performed on the muzzle images
which are partially occluded (e.g., percentage (%) of occluded part of a muzzle point
image pattern). We then applied these occluded muzzle point images and performed
the recognition approach to investigate whether proposed texture feature extraction
based approach is robust against the occluded of muzzle point images pattern, while
classifying the muzzle point features for recognition purpose of the cattle. The
recognition of cattle using muzzle point images is considered different rotations under
occlusion classes. These classes are divided according to occluded part of muzzle


Table 7 Classification accuracy for (classes A, B and C) muzzle point pattern, each group has different set of
with muzzle point images

Classification accuracy (%)

Classification        Class A (200 cattle)          Class B (200 subjects)        Class C (100 subjects)

Rotation (in degree) 00         150          450    900       1350      1800      2250      2700      3150
Subjects             50         50           100    50        50        100       20        40        40

K-NN                  95.82% 93.87% 90.74% 92.34% 90.72% 92.85% 83.62% 85.83% 89.95%
Fuzzy-K-NN            85.75% 87.65% 89.56% 84.25% 83.75% 82.73% 74.35% 72.67% 76.26%
RBF                   81.75% 84.96% 86.25% 70.36% 72.86% 74.65% 78.27% 76.98% 79.98%
DT                    78.83% 73.23% 79.54% 71.65% 72.45% 79.45% 81.47% 85.68% 85.76%
GMM                   69.75% 70.25% 71.63% 73.42% 76.25% 78.47% 68.74% 70.65% 72.47%
PNN                   90.73% 92.89% 94.56% 89.42% 91.72% 92.88% 78.42% 79.42% 80.68%
MLP                   62.73% 64.47% 67.84% 72.33% 73.97% 75.93% 68.73% 70.92% 71.84%

26574                                                       Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


point images (bottom part and vertical part of muzzle images) (occlusion are defined
in the given percentage) as shown in Table 8.
    In this experiment, we have used 60% of the total database of muzzle images for
training the classification models and remaining muzzle images are applied as test
occluded muzzle images (e.g., different occluded percentage (%) and sizes). After
training the classification models, we applied remaining occluded images for the testing and
classifying individual cattle. The classification accuracy of occluded muzzle point images is
shown in Table 8.


8 Discussion

In this paper, we have demonstrated the comparative study of experimental results for
identifying and classifying the cattle using texture features of muzzle point images.
The summary of cattle recognition, and classification techniques based on muzzle
images pattern with their accuracy are shown in Table 9. In the similar direction,
Noviyanto et al. [49] proposed a technique for refinement of matching in SIFT
descriptor based approach for identification of cattle and reported the minimum Equal
Error Rate (EER) value of 0.0167. This EER value was less than obtained EER values of
original SIFT technique.
   The author Tharawat et al. [62] proposed a method for the recognition of cattle using Local
Binary Pattern (LBP) descriptor based features to classify the cattle breeds (races) using local
features of the muzzle image pattern in Table 9.
   The author of [45], Minagawa et al. proposed a recognition approach for cattle using bead
features of images of muzzle print. The extracted features of bead are classified and recognized
using unsupervised learning Principal Component Analysis (PCA) based on the co-occurrence
feature matrix of beads features for calculation of eigenvalues for cattle recognition and yield
30–40% of recognition accuracy. The author of [48], Noviyanto et al. proposed a method for
cattle identification using Speeded Up Robust Feature from the images of muzzle print of cattle.
   The author of [4], Awad et al. proposed a SIFT descriptor based approach for identification
of cattle using 48 muzzle print images which was captured with black ink on A-5 white paper
using manual processes. The printed muzzle images on A-5 paper have very low quality
images, blurred, and poor eliminations. Therefore, this approach was not robust for recognition
of cattle in various cabarets challenges such as low illumination, low quality (resolution).In

Table 8 Classification accuracy for (Classes A, B and C) groups of muzzle point pattern, each groups have
different number of muzzle point images

Classification accuracy (%)

Classifiers Subjects   Occluded images (Bottom portion)          Occluded images (Top portion)
                       (Class A, 200)                            (Class B (200) + C (100))

                       50        50         50        50         75        75         75         75

K-NN                   92.78%    94.87%     96.84%    97.34%     98.85%     96.65%    85.82%     91.82%
Naive Bayes            95.85%    97.75%     98.56%    88.95%     89.25%     94.73%    74.35%     76.26%
PNN                    97.86%    98.57%     97.45%    89.39%     94.55%     99.94%    89.72%     94.72%
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                              26575

Table 9 Summary of cattle recognition and classification techniques based on muzzle images pattern used in the
literatures and experimental results of proposed approach

Authors                    Images       Features       Recognition approach     Accuracy (%)

1. Noviyanto et al. [49]   48           Muzzle         SIFT                     0.0167(EER)
2. Barry et al. [6]        100          Beads          Euclidean distance       45%
                                                         classifier
3. Minagawa et al. [45]    43           Beads          PCA                      30–40%
4. Tharawat et al. [62]    217          LBP            LDA, SVM                 89%
5. Kim et al. [29]         12           Cattle Face    ANMA                     80%
6. Cai et al. [9]          30           LBP            RASL + WLBP              95.30%
7. Noviyanto et al. [48]   15           SURF           Eigen face               90%
8. Awad et al. [4]         15           SIFT           SIFT + RANSAC            93.33%
9. Corkery et al. [71]     50 (sheep)   PCA            PCA+ ICA+ Cosine         96%
                                                         distance
10. Proposed approach      5000         Hybrid texture (K-NN, Fuzzy-KNN,        (96.74%) (For K-NN
                                                         DT, PNN, RBF,            classification), (94.56%)
                                                         MLP Naive Bayes)         (Fuzzy-KNN classification)

Where, EER Equal Error Rate, ANMA Associate Neural Memory Algorithm, WLBP Webers Local Binary Pattern
Descriptor) + RASL + Chi square distance, RANSAC RANdom Sample Consensus algorithm, SIFT Scale
Invariant Feature Transform, SURF Speeded Up Robust Features, SVM Support Vector Machine classifier,
PCA Principal Component Analysis, ICA Independent Component Analysis, LBP Local Binary Pattern



this paper, we have proposed novel hybrid feature extraction approaches for recognition of
cattle using their muzzle point image pattern.
   We have extracted the texture features from the 5000 muzzle point image pattern and extracted
features are classified using the K-NN, Fuzzy-KNN, DT, PNN, RBF, MLP and Naive Bayes
classification models. The K-NN and Fuzzy K-NN classification methods yield the recognition
accuracy of 96.74%, and 94.56%, respectively. The proposed approach yields 96.74% classifi-
cation accuracy using K-NN classification technique. It can be observed from the comparative
experimental results (shown in Table 9) that none of the previously proposed approaches using
texture feature based techniques yields the better results as compared to proposed approach to
recognition the cattle using muzzle point image pattern. In this paper, the performance of the
proposed method has been compared quantitatively with other current state-of-the-art methods
viz. recognition method used by Tharawat et al. [62], Noviyanto et al. [48], Awad et al. [4],
Minagawa et al. [45], Noviyanto et al. [49], and Barry et al. [6] (as shown in Table 9).

8.1 Analysis of proposed muzzle point recognition approach

The time complexity of proposed cattle recognition approach can be high during similarity
matching of muzzle point features of individual cattle which may thwart the application of
cattle recognition system in real-time. The main reasons are (1) The muzzle point image
database has been captured from the unconstrained environment, such as low illumination,
poor image quality, blurred, head movement and body posture of cattle for the recognition of
individual cattle, (2) pre-processing and feature extraction steps can consume more time for the
recognition of cattle, (3) identification (one to many matching process) step can take more time
by matching of test image with stored muzzle point image database.
26576                                                      Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580


    In the proposed cattle recognition approach, finally a 2-D feature matrix of 5000 × 186
texture features was constructed using all the extracted set of texture features, where 5000 is
the number of muzzle point images pattern in the cattle database and 186 are the total number
of extracted texture features from the muzzle point images.
    During identification (one to many matching process) of individual cattle, matching of simi-
larity score (based on matching of Euclidean distances between feature vectors) of a test muzzle
point image is compared with stored muzzle point image database. Therefore, it can take more time
to identify the individual cattle by matching of muzzle point features in real time scenario.


9 Conclusions and future directions

In this paper, we present a hybrid texture features extraction approach for recognition, and
classification of cattle using their muzzle point image pattern. The proposed approach operates
on the texture feature vectors obtained from the 5000 images of muzzle image pattern using eight
texture features approaches and performs classification, and recognition of cattle. The proposed
approach yields the better recognition accuracies of 94.5%, and 96.74% by classifying the extracted
texture features of muzzle point images by applying K-NN, and Fuzzy-K-NN classification
techniques. This research work illustrates a current state-of–art approach for the recognition, and
classification of individual cattle in the animal biometrics, pattern recognition, and computer vision.
   The proposed hybrid feature extraction based automatic recognition of cattle based on
muzzle point image pattern extracts texture features from the different classes such as, A, B,
and C of muzzle image database. The experimental results indicate that the proposed approach
succeeds in efficiently utilizing a feature representation of input data to perform the accurate
classification, and recognition of muzzle point pattern recognition for individual cattle.
   The proposed approach can provide the better solutions to problems of registration, missed,
swapped, false insurance claims, and traceability of cattle in the traditional animal recognition
based methodologies, and livestock framework based systems.
   From the achieved experimental evaluation on a database of 5000 muzzle point image
pattern of cattle (e.g., 500 cattle (subjects) 10 images of each subject), and their qualitative, and
quantitative analysis, it can be concluded that the proposed hybrid texture features approach is
performing better in comparison to other cattle recognition technique. It illustrates that the
automatic recognition of cattle is feasible using muzzle point image pattern as primary animal
biometric characteristics. As well as, it also capable of alleviating the problems associated with
covariates of muzzle images due to low illumination, poor image quality, and pose variation
due to head movement of cattle.
   After performance evaluations of different classification and recognition algorithms to
recognition individual cattle based on muzzle image pattern database, we hereby conclude
that various multidisciplinary researchers, scientists, developers have yet to explore the depths
level of animal monitoring, tracking, registration, and control and outbreak of critical diseases
of animal by recognizing individual animal. In future, it can be planned to do further research
keeping in view the following areas:

1. The future research work will put emphasis on increasing the size of muzzle point pattern
   database. The database will be enhanced, and different conditions will be considered while
   the acquisition of muzzle point images for each cattle (subjects): low illumination,
   blurriness, and pose variations as image covariates in the muzzle point image database.
Multimed Tools Appl (2017) 76:26551–26580                                                                         26577


   We are currently working on preparing an animal biometric based recognition system for
   the verification of false insurance claims of various valuable animal, registration, and
   health monitoring process using their animal biometric characteristics.
2. The multi-modality based animal recognition based approaches can be developed to en-
   hanced the classification, and recognition accuracy of individual cattle by applying muzzle
   point image pattern, and face images of cattle as primary animal biometric characteristics.
3. A real-time animal biometric based recognition system can be designed and developed
   applying advanced computer vision, and pattern recognition algorithms for the recognition
   of individual cattle.

Mr. Santosh Kumar completed his B. Tech. in Department of Computer Science and Engineering from Ajay
Kumar Garg Engineering, Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh Technical University (UPTU) in 2008 and M. Tech. in
Computer Science & Engineering from Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Ranchi (Jharkhand, India) in 2012.
Currently he is pursuing Ph. D. under supervision of Dr. Sanjay Kumar Singh, (Associate professor, Deptt. CSE,
IIT (BHU), Varanasi) from the Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology
(B.H.U.) Varanasi. His research interests span a wide range of spectrum including Computer vision, Pattern
recognition, Image Processing, video processing, Swarm Intelligence, Bio-inspired computing, Artificial Intel-
ligence, operating systems and theoretical computer science. He is a received several best paper and best poster
awards and the NVIDIA Innovation Award in 2015. Currently, he was a recipient of best poster awards for the
research exhibition on Institute Day-2016, IIT (BHU), Varanasi, India. He is member of the Computer Society
and the Association for Computing Machinery.




Dr. Sanjay Kumar Singh received his B. Tech. in Computer Engg., M. Tech. in Computer Applications and
Ph.D. degrees in computer science from Veer Bahadur Singh Purvanchal University Jaunpur, Uttar Pradesh,
respectively. He is currently an Associate Professor at the Department of Computer Science and Engineering,
Indian Institute of Technology (B.H.U.), Varanasi-221,005, India. His areas of interest are biometrics, image
processing, computer vision, multimedia, biometric encryption, and information fusion. He is a Certified Novell
Engineer (CNE) from Novell Netware, USA and a Certified Novell Administrator (CNA) from Novell Netware,
USA. He is a member of LIMSTE, IEE, International Association of Engineers and ISCE. He has over 100
national and international journal publications, book chapters and conference papers. He is a member of the
IEEE, and Computer Society and the Association for Computing Machinery.
